channel.title: Posts on RUI SHANG
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on RUI SHANG
channel.copyright: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

item.title: Airbnb London
item.description: Description of Data Data type The data is sourced from the Inside Airbnb , a publicly Airbnb data provider. The dataset comprises of three main tables: listings - Detailed listings attributes including price (continuous), location, room_type (categorical), neighbourhood (categorical), ratings (continuous) among others. reviews - Detailed reviews given by the guests with 6 attributes. Key attributes include date (datetime), listing_id (discrete), reviewer_id (discrete) and comment (textual). calendar - Provides details about booking for the next year by listing.
item.content:
Description of Data

Data type

The data is sourced from the Inside Airbnb , a publicly Airbnb data provider. The dataset comprises of three main tables:

listings - Detailed listings attributes including price (continuous), location, room_type (categorical), neighbourhood (categorical), ratings (continuous) among others. reviews - Detailed reviews given by the guests with 6 attributes. Key attributes include date (datetime), listing_id (discrete), reviewer_id (discrete) and comment (textual). calendar - Provides details about booking for the next year by listing. Four attributes in total including listing_id (discrete), date (datetime), available (categorical) and price (continuous).]

Analysis

A few imputations and transformations are made to create the desired visualizations. There were no major inconsistencies or mismatches in the data, but most of the columns/features The required format should be checked and hence were manipulated in a way that their meanings are retained.

Generate static files

$ hexo generate

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

$ hexo deploy

More info: Deployment

item.title: Does Brexit impact housing market?
item.description: Introcution On 23 June 2016, the United Kingdom voted on a referendum to leave the European Union. Pundits and scholars alike attempted to predict the immediate economic impact of “Brexit”, with varying degrees of success. An analysis published by the Treasury in May 2016 indicated that market uncertainty due to Brexit would lead to an increase in lending costs, thus leading to a decrease in overall house prices. Within two years, house prices were projected to fall between 10-18% - a statistic hungrily reported by the media as both a catastrophe and an opportunity .
item.content:
Introcution

On 23 June 2016, the United Kingdom voted on a referendum to leave the European Union. Pundits and scholars alike attempted to predict the immediate economic impact of “Brexit”, with varying degrees of success. An analysis published by the Treasury in May 2016 indicated that market uncertainty due to Brexit would lead to an increase in lending costs, thus leading to a decrease in overall house prices. Within two years, house prices were projected to fall between 10-18% - a statistic hungrily reported by the media as both a catastrophe and an opportunity .

This analysis investigates the short term consequences of Brexit on median house price and overall number of house sales in London. In particular, we seek to answer the following question: To what extent has Brexit impacted the spatial distribution of London house sales?

To address this question, we attempt to model house sales during a one month period four moths after Brexit using data from the three months prior to the referendum. We present the results of a simple linear model and a geographically weighted regression (GWR) alongside several visualizations. Finally, we discuss consequences of our findings and suggest further questions for study.

###Distription of Data

This analysis relies exclusively on the HM Land Registry Price Paid Dataset [9], boundaries provided by the London Data Store [10], and postcode centroids from the Ordinance Survey [11]. Data is used under the UK Open Government License. We discuss the following considerations with respect to the housing price data: completeness, outliers, and included fields.

First, the data is notably incomplete. As Figure 1 on page 2 shows, there are no observations within the borough of Westminster, which houses much of London’s most expensive property. The reason for this is omission unclear. In addition, some types of transactions are not included in the dataset, such as divorce settlements, partial property sales, or gifts. Transactions with prices specifically including VAT are omitted.

Only one spatial outlier was identified within the dataset. Shown in red in Figure 1, this point is clearly outside the boundary of London and was removed from our analysis.

​ Figure 1, Completeness and spatial outliers within the housing price dataset.

​ Figure 2, Outlier

Analysis

The analysis consisted of five phases: ingest, data processing, visualization, linear modeling, and GWR. It was conducted on both the ward and borough level. All analysis and visualization was conducted in R (see Code Appendix).

House price data were ingested from a csv/text file into R as a dataframe. These individual observations were then assigned the Easting and Northing (in BNG) of their containing post- code and converted to a SpatialDataFrame. Subsets of these points were selected based on transaction date for each of the four months before and after the referendum. Using a spatial overlay, points within each borough were selected and summary statistics (count, min, max, mean, median) were appended to the borough data set. Count was scaled by borough area. For each borough, 40 additional pieces of information were appended (8 timeslices and 5 statistics). The process was repeated at the ward level.

Since the process of conveying a home takes an average of eight weeks in the UK [12, 13, 14], a one month time period1 beginning four months after the referendum was selected as the post- Brexit comparison period. Although the time required to purchase a home from decision to close may exceed four months for some buyers, four months after the decision nevertheless provides a reasonable heuristic for transactions which began after the referendum occurred.

To visualize potential trends, we plot both pre- and post-Brexit sales volume together in a bivariate choropleth, grouped by tertiles (see Figure 3). Purple boroughs remained in the same sales tertile after Brexit, while teal boroughs decreased and and pink boroughs.

Figure 3, A bivariate choropleth map2 showing the relationship between number

​ of house sales before and after Brexit in London boroughs.

increased. This map suggests that borough-level sales were unlikely to be different four months after the referendum results compared with one month before.

To further investigate the hypothesis that the spatial distribution of sales did not significantly change, we created linear and GWR models to predict post-Brexit sales based on pre-Brexit sales data. If the spatial distribution did change, we would expect pre-Brexit data to provide a poor model (low r2 value) for post-Brexit data.

As Figure 3 shows, the difference in performance between the linear model (e.g. model <- lm(borough$p4_number ∼ borough$b1_number + borough$b2_number + borough$b3_number)) and the corresponding GWR was on the order of ∼ 10−3. Due to missing data GWR was not computed for median price. Since this method did not provide significant improvement over a linear model, it is possible the underlying spatial distribution of house sale volume did not significantly change before and after Brexit.

The stark difference between the high linear model multiple r2 at borough level and the compar- atively low r2 at ward level provides an interesting example of the modifiable areal unit problem. Due to the size of some wards, total number of house sales in a given month may be extremely low. Such small sample sizes require careful statistical consideration. To avoid this concern, we have chosen to visualize and discuss data at the borough level. The high r2 values from our models suggest that house sales from the three months prior to the referendum explain 93% of the variability in number and mean value of house sales after the referendum, and that spatial distribution of polygons may not be significant.

​ Table 1, Summary of GWR and linear models

​ Borough (b) and Ward (w) Number of sales (_) and Median price ($)

There are a number of factors that might account for the remaining 7% variation. For example, seasonal trends impact house sales; nearly triple the average number of houses are sold in the last week of March (right before Tax Day), while fewer than average houses are sold in the last two weeks of December (presumably due to the holidays).

To understand the spatial performance of these linear models, we plot and examine the residuals, as shown in Figure 4 on page 5. (Residuals for median value are in pounds sterling.) No pattern is immediately obvious. This suggests that unexplained variation in house sales may be due to a non-spatial variable, such as seasonal effects.

item.title: Excise for Bubble Chart
item.description: Import Data #Import China shapefile: china_map<-readOGR("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/DataWarehouse-master/Rstudy /CHN_adm/bou2_4p.shp")data1<- china_map@data data2<- data.frame(id=row.names(data1),data1) china_map1 <- fortify(china_map)#Transffom and join the data: china_map1 <- fortify(china_map) china_map_data <- join(china_map1,data2, type = "full") mydata <- read.csv("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/drnum.ke.csv") china_data <- join(china_map_data, mydata, type="full") #Get the province coordinates : province_city <- read.csv("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/chinaprovince.ke.csv ") # example 1 : Point chart on map ggplot(china_data,aes(long,lat))+ geom_polygon(aes(group=group),fill="white",colour="grey60")+ geom_point(data=province_city,aes(x=jd,y=wd),colour="red")+ coord_map("polyconic") + theme( panel.grid = element_blank(), panel.background = element_blank(), axis.text = element_blank(), axis.
item.content:
Import Data

#Import China shapefile:
china_map<-readOGR("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/DataWarehouse-master/Rstudy /CHN_adm/bou2_4p.shp")
 data1<- china_map@data
data2<- data.frame(id=row.names(data1),data1)
china_map1 <- fortify(china_map)
#Transffom and join the data: china_map1 <- fortify(china_map)
china_map_data <- join(china_map1,data2, type = "full")

mydata <- read.csv("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/drnum.ke.csv")
china_data <- join(china_map_data, mydata, type="full")

#Get the province coordinates :
province_city <- read.csv("/Users/apple/Desktop/CASA/GIS/As 1/chinaprovince.ke.csv ")
# example 1 : Point chart on map
ggplot(china_data,aes(long,lat))+
     geom_polygon(aes(group=group),fill="white",colour="grey60")+
     geom_point(data=province_city,aes(x=jd,y=wd),colour="red")+
     coord_map("polyconic") +
     theme(
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks = element_blank(),
          axis.title = element_blank()
          )

province_city$Dct_nur<-NULL
province_city$Dct_nur<-rnorm(31,100,50)
province_city$EN_NAME<-NULL
province_city$EN_NAME<-rnorm(31,100,50)
# example 2 : Bubble chart on map
ggplot()+
     geom_polygon(data=china_data,aes(x=long,y=lat,group=group),fill="grey95",colo
ur="grey80")+
     geom_point(data=province_city,aes(x=jd,y=wd,size=Dct_nur),shape=21,fill="#8E0
F2E",colour="black",alpha=0.4)+
     scale_size_area(max_size=8)+
     coord_map("polyconic") +
     guides(size=guide_legend(reverse=TRUE,title=NULL))+
     ggtitle("Numbers of doctor nand nurse per 1000 persons in China")+
     theme(
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks = element_blank(),
          axis.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position =c(0.15,0.4),
          legend.background=element_rect(colour="white",fill="white"),
          legend.text.align=1
)

# Exmple 3: Bubble Chart with name
ggplot()+
     geom_polygon(data=china_data,aes(x=long,y=lat,group=group),fill="grey95",colo
ur="grey80")+
     geom_point(data=province_city,aes(x=jd,y=wd,size=Dct_nur),shape=21,fill="#8E0
F2E",colour="black",alpha=0.4)+
     scale_size_area(max_size=6)+
     coord_map("polyconic") +
     geom_text(aes(x=jd+2.3,y=wd,label=proname),size =1.5,fontface="plain",data=pr
ovince_city) +
     guides(size=guide_legend(reverse=TRUE,title=NULL))+
     ggtitle("Numbers of doctor nand nurse per 1000 persons in China")+
     theme(
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks = element_blank(),
          axis.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position =c(0.15,0.4),
          legend.background=element_rect(colour="white",fill="white"),
          legend.text.align=1
)

item.title: The Spatial Distribution Character of Starbucks in Shanghai
item.description: Introduction The aim of this article is to analysis the spatial distribution characteristics of Starbucks, the retail business coffee shop, in Shanghai. For the purpose of this analysis ArcGIS software is used. The data employed in this study include the number of Starbucks stores in Shanghai, their addresses, geographical coordinates and their Shanghai administrative districts. Store location data is derived from Opendatasoft.com and the Shanghai geometric shapefile from the ArcGIS official website.
item.content:
Introduction

The aim of this article is to analysis the spatial distribution characteristics of Starbucks, the retail business coffee shop, in Shanghai. For the purpose of this analysis ArcGIS software is used. The data employed in this study include the number of Starbucks stores in Shanghai, their addresses, geographical coordinates and their Shanghai administrative districts. Store location data is derived from Opendatasoft.com and the Shanghai geometric shapefile from the ArcGIS official website. Through the combination of different software and geostaticmethods, this report gives the general distribution characteristics of Shanghai’s Starbucks stores and the general distribution regulations behind them.

Methodology

Proximity analysis and Geostatistics tools are mainly used to explore the spatial distribution characteristics and location selection factors of Shanghai’s Starbucks stores. Firstly, the spatial distribution of Starbucks is shown with a heat map and studied using the nearest neighbour distance analysis and Ripley’s K function method. Secondly, buffer analysis is used to explore spatial distribution and service coverage. The location of a store is classified by spatial function in order to analyse its distribution structure. Finally, the standard deviational ellipse is used to analyse the spatial distribution direction of Starbucks.

Average Nearest Neighbour Analysis

This measures the degree of distribution dispersion, using a hypothesis testing method. It is derived from the ratio of the actual observed average distance R between the nearest pairs of points to the ideal average distance RE in the ideal random distribution model. According to the output of ArcGIS tools, whether the distribution of Shanghai Starbucks stores is uniform is determined.

Ripley’s K function

Ripley’s K function (multi-distance spatial clustering analysis) can show how the clustering or discretisation of point elements change relative to different distances. The function is given as (Bailey et al.,1995):

It shows how spatial aggregation or discretion of location changes when the neighbourhood range is modified. This study uses Ripley’s K function to analyse the basic distribution pattern characteristics of Starbucks stores in Shanghai.

Buffer analysis

Buffer analysis is used to determine the spatial proximity of different geographical locations. In this paper, it is used to measure the spatial aggregation and distribution pattern of Starbucks stores. However, there may be other reasons for the spatial distribution of Starbucks stores, such as transportation or commercial layout. According to the location information in the data, Starbucks store locations are classified by their spatial function. The research will combine this classification with buffer analysis in order to carry out an in-depth study of Starbucks spatial functional distribution structure.

Standard deviational ellipse

Standard deviational ellipse can understand the scattering range and direction of data. This method is widely used in the study of virus transmission and air pollution diffusion. According to the distribution of data, the standard deviational ellipse tool in ArcGIS can generate three ellipsis regions. It shows that in normal distribution, 68%, 95% and 99.7% of the data values will fall within one, two or three standard deviations, respectively. In this report, the direction of Starbucks stores data distribution is addressed by combining the data scatter plot with the long and short axis of the ellipse.

Spatial Distribution Model

​ Figure 1. The Spatial Distribution of Starbucks Stores in Shanghai The spatial layout of Starbucks stores in Shanghai are shown in Figure 1. They present the characteristics of a cohesive distribution pattern. In Table 1, we can see that 66% of the 497 Starbucks stores in Shanghai are located in the districts of Pudong, Xuhui, Huangpu, Jing’an and Changning. In this paper, the 497 Starbucks stores in Shanghai are first analysed using Nearest Neighbour Analysis, a hypothesis testing-based method in ArcGIS. The zerohypothesis is the default as the distribution of Starbucks is random and uniform. In Table 2, the Z-score and the p-value are measures of statistical significance, which are required to determine whether a zero hypothesis is rejected or not. According to the Z score and p value in Table 2, there is sufficient evidence to negate the original hypothesis.

​ Table 2. The Nearest Neighbour Ratio of Shanghai Starbucks The nearest neighbour ratio R of Starbucks stores is 825.13⁄5096.10=0.16. Its R value is far less than 1, indicating that the spatial layout of Shanghai Starbucks stores is in a cohesive distribution pattern.

​ Figure 2. The L Function of Starbucks Stores in Shanghai

Ripley’s K function is used for further exploration of the distribution characteristics. Figure 2 shows the distance on the horizontal axis and the value of the L function on the vertical axis. If the specific distance L(d) is larger than the expected value (blue line), the cluster in degree of the distribution is higher than that of the random distribution of the distance (analysis scale). The observation value is larger than the upper limit of the confidence interval, the spatial clustering of the distance has statistical significance. The graph shows that the L(d) value is much greater than expected, which indicates that Starbucks stores are clustered in the urban area of Shanghai and that there is no evidence of random or uniform distribution in this scale.

Spatial Functional Structure of Shanghai Starbucks

In order to be able to discuss the regulations behind the clustered distribution, the spatial distribution of Starbucks stores in Shanghai are visualised and analysed with the help of buffer analysis and a spatial functional classification of the stores’ locations. Before analysing the functional structure of Starbucks’s space, the spatial distribution density of its stores in Shanghai are analysed using point density analysis tools. In Figure 3, the spatial distribution density of the Starbucks stores shows a gradually decreasing circular structure going from the city centre to the outer city limits. The districts of Huangpu, Jing’an and Xuhui are in the high-density areas of the Starbucks spatial distribution pattern and are also at the centre of the circular structure.

​ Figure 3. The Buffer Analysis of Starbucks Stores in Guangzhou. In Figure 3, the service

areas of the stores overlap in the downtown area. This phenomenon is perhaps due to the high-density shopping malls and the number of offices in the city centre as well as the spatial and functional structure of the traffic area. To further study this phenomenon, store locations are classified according to their functions in a CSV file with a dataset based on keyword search. In Figure 4, the classification results are visualised.

​ Figure 4. The Functional Structure of Starbucks Stores in Shanghai

Figure 4 shows that the distribution of Starbucks clustered in some certain functional areas. All Starbucks in Shanghai are classified according to location information.

The Distribution Direction of Shanghai Starbucks Stores

The location distribution of retail stores often has directionality, which means that the degree of dispersion in each direction is different. The standard deviational ellipse tool in ArcGIS is used to describe the difference in the degree of dispersion in the spatial distribution direction of Shanghai Starbucks stores. In Figure 6, 99.7% Starbucks stores in Shanghai show a spatial deviation from the northeast and southwest directions.

Shanghai is a coastal city, so Starbucks is influenced by the regional shape of the city’s urban area, which can only develop by moving inland and explains, in part, the spatial distribution of Shanghai. Starbucks’s spatial distribution direction is also based on Shanghai’s urban development planning.

​ Figure 6. The Standard Deviational Ellipse of Shanghai Starbucks Stores

Figure 6 shows that the main expansion axes (the longer axis of the eclipse) are concentrated in the direction of Baoshan, Minhang and Jiading, while the expansion of Pudong is not as pronounced. This is due to it being close to the sea. The spatial distribution pattern of the Starbucks stores is also consistent with Shanghai’s urban planning strategy. The policy plan is to build a new downtown area in the Jiading District. This area is in Starbucks’s main expansion axes (as shown in Figure 7).

​ Figure 7. Shanghai Planning Strategy (Li et al., 2003)

In accordance with the urban planning strategy, the direction of the stores’ extension in Shanghai’s new downtown area also shows the same functional feature, and objectively confirms the trend of the spatial functional character of Starbucks distribution.
